figures.caption: Table 1: Performance comparison on MIG-Bench. Best non-human results are shown in bold.
Table 5: Representative query templates used in CGC.

Figure 6: A qualitative example on BLINK. The task requires selecting the point in the second image that best corresponds to a reference point in the first image. CGC identifies the correct correspondence, whereas the base model is misled by an incorrect analogy.
Table 2: Performance comparison on VLM2-Bench. Best non-human results are shown in bold.

Figure 5: Qualitative examples on VLM2-Bench. Top: fine-grained cross-image change attribution. CGC correctly determines that the bananas are not the object whose attribute changed, while the base model incorrectly attributes the change to them. Bottom: object-centric attribute change reasoning. CGC correctly recognizes the cat’s state transition across the two images, whereas the base model fails on this fine-grained comparison.

Figure 9: A qualitative example on MMMU (Part II). Continuation of the MMMU case in Figure 8. CGC better aligns the visual graph pattern with the required scientific relationship and finally selects the correct answer, whereas the base model follows a less reliable reasoning path.

Figure 4: Qualitative examples on MIG-Bench. Top: object constancy across an image sequence. CGC correctly tracks the same vehicle across frames and produces more accurate grounding in the queried image, whereas the base model fails to preserve identity-consistent localization. Bottom: common-object grounding under cluttered cross-image contexts. CGC better suppresses cross-image attention leakage and identifies the actual shared object category, while the base model is distracted by locally salient but incorrect objects.
Table 4: Ablation studies on various configurations. The best results are shown in bold.
Table 3: Performance on general vision benchmarks. Best non-human results are shown in bold.
Figure 3: Data scaling law. Performance improves steadily across five benchmarks as synthesized grounding data scales from 6k to 36k. The blue line denotes the average score.

Figure 2: Overview of the proposed CGC framework. Starting from single-image grounding annotations, CGC automatically constructs multi-image training instances through Automated Contrastive Data Synthesis, which includes Inter-Image Contrast for decoupled distractor contexts and Intra-Image Contrast for correlated cross-view grounding. Under a Think-before-Grounding paradigm, the model first produces a reasoning trace and then outputs a structured grounding prediction. Finally, Rule-Based Spatial Reward optimization under GRPO jointly improves source-image attribution, spatial alignment, and output validity.

Figure 10: A qualitative example on MMStar. The task requires solving a visual analogy under 3D structural transformation. CGC correctly captures the transformation pattern and selects the right option, whereas the base model is distracted by partial local similarity and predicts an incorrect answer.

Figure 8: A qualitative example on MMMU (Part I). The task asks which candidate graph best matches the physical relationship between the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons and incident-light frequency. This page shows the question, candidate graphs, and the beginning of the model responses.

Figure 7: Qualitative examples on HallusionBench and MathVista. Top: in HallusionBench, CGC correctly judges that the two queried gray regions have the same size, whereas the base model makes an incorrect spatial comparison. Bottom: in MathVista, CGC correctly reasons over a food-web diagram and identifies the organism most affected by the disappearance of clams, while the base model follows an incomplete evidence chain.
